title: The cobbler's son
summary: Marco Bellini has mended shoes in Bologna for forty-one years. His son is a doctor at the main hospital. People keep asking the wrong question.
body:
Marco Bellini repairs shoes in a small workshop in Bologna. His workshop is on Via San Felice, between a fruit shop and a barber. He is sixty-two. He has been a cobbler for forty-one years, since he started as an apprentice for his father at twenty-one. His tools are old. Some of them are older than he is. The leather smell of the workshop has not changed since 1962.

Marco has one son, Davide. Davide is a doctor now. He is a pulmonologist at the main hospital in Bologna — a specialist in lungs. He is thirty-four. He drives a small car. He owns a flat near the hospital. He often works fourteen-hour shifts.

People sometimes ask Marco, in the way that people do, whether he is proud. They mean: are you proud that your son left the workshop and became a doctor? It is a question that assumes the answer.

Marco gives the same answer every time. He says, 'I am proud of Davide. I have always been proud. Now let me tell you something my father told me, and I think it is the right thing to say.' He pauses, often, before saying the next part. 'A son becoming a doctor is not better than a father being a cobbler. They are different things. Some sons would have made bad doctors and good cobblers. Some cobblers would have made bad fathers. I do not measure children with a ruler that was given to me by other people.'

This is more careful than it sounds. Marco is not denying that the doctor's life is harder to get to, or better paid, or more visible in the town. He is denying a comparison. He is refusing to say that one life is a step above the other.

Davide and Marco still see each other every Sunday. Davide brings bread. Marco brings wine. They eat in the workshop, with the door closed, because the workshop is the quietest room either of them has. They do not talk about shoes or lungs. They talk about a cousin who is moving back from Milan, about Marco's mother who is now eighty-eight, about a neighbour's leaking roof.

When Davide leaves, Marco often sits for a few minutes at the bench. He looks at a particular pair of his son's old shoes on a shelf. He has kept them since Davide was sixteen. He says he is not sentimental, but he keeps them. They are the proof, perhaps, of his small theory: that he was never raising a doctor. He was raising a person who, among other things, became a doctor.

Then Marco goes back to the shoe he was mending. There is always one more shoe.

annotations.meaningVi: thợ học việc / người học nghề
(nghĩa đen) cây thước; (nghĩa bóng) thang đo / cách đánh giá
uỷ mị / sướt mướt theo lối tình cảm

grammarNotes.title: Comparatives in argument: 'better than', 'more X than', 'a step above'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong văn nghị luận, các cấu trúc so sánh đóng vai trò quan trọng — và phủ nhận một so sánh cũng mạnh không kém việc khẳng định. Trong bài: 'A son becoming a doctor is not better than a father being a cobbler.' (phủ định so sánh) 'the doctor's life is harder to get to, or better paid, or more visible' (so sánh thường — thừa nhận một chiều) 'one life is a step above the other' (ẩn dụ so sánh). Marco vừa thừa nhận khác biệt thực tế (better paid…) vừa từ chối thang đo đạo đức (not better than).
grammarNotes.bodyEn: In argument, comparative structures matter — and refusing a comparison is as strong as making one. 'A son becoming a doctor is not better than a father being a cobbler.' (negated comparison) 'The doctor's life is harder to get to, or better paid, or more visible.' (granted comparison along practical axes) 'One life is a step above the other.' (a metaphorical comparison). Marco grants the practical differences while denying the moral ranking.

translationVi:
Marco Bellini sửa giày trong một xưởng nhỏ ở Bologna. Xưởng của ông nằm trên phố Via San Felice, giữa một tiệm hoa quả và một tiệm cắt tóc. Ông sáu mươi hai tuổi. Ông là thợ sửa giày bốn mươi mốt năm rồi, kể từ khi bắt đầu học việc với cha mình lúc hai mươi mốt tuổi. Đồ nghề của ông cũ. Một số còn già hơn cả ông. Mùi da trong xưởng không thay đổi từ năm 1962.

Marco có một người con trai tên Davide. Davide bây giờ là bác sĩ. Anh là bác sĩ chuyên khoa phổi ở bệnh viện chính của Bologna — chuyên về phổi. Anh ba mươi tư tuổi. Anh đi một chiếc xe nhỏ. Anh sở hữu một căn hộ gần bệnh viện. Anh thường làm ca mười bốn giờ.

Đôi khi người ta hỏi Marco, theo kiểu người ta vẫn hỏi, rằng ông có tự hào không. Họ muốn nói: ông có tự hào vì con trai đã rời xưởng và trở thành bác sĩ không? Đó là câu hỏi tự nó đã giả định sẵn câu trả lời.

Lần nào Marco cũng trả lời như nhau. Ông nói: 'Tôi tự hào về Davide. Tôi luôn tự hào về cháu. Bây giờ hãy để tôi kể điều cha tôi đã nói với tôi, và tôi nghĩ nó đúng.' Ông thường dừng một lát trước khi nói tiếp. 'Việc một người con trở thành bác sĩ không hơn việc một người cha là thợ sửa giày. Đó là hai chuyện khác nhau. Có những đứa con sẽ là bác sĩ tệ nhưng thợ sửa giày giỏi. Có những thợ sửa giày sẽ là người cha tệ. Tôi không đo con cái bằng một cây thước người khác đưa cho tôi.'

Điều này thận trọng hơn nó nghe. Marco không phủ nhận rằng đời bác sĩ khó với tới hơn, lương cao hơn, hoặc dễ thấy hơn trong thành phố. Ông đang phủ nhận một sự so sánh. Ông từ chối nói rằng đời này là 'một bậc cao hơn' đời kia.

Davide và Marco vẫn gặp nhau mỗi Chủ nhật. Davide mang bánh mì. Marco mang rượu. Họ ăn trong xưởng, đóng cửa, vì xưởng là căn phòng yên tĩnh nhất mà cả hai có. Họ không nói về giày hay về phổi. Họ nói về một người anh họ đang chuyển từ Milan về, về mẹ của Marco năm nay tám mươi tám tuổi, về mái nhà dột của hàng xóm.

Khi Davide đi, Marco thường ngồi vài phút bên bàn làm việc. Ông nhìn một đôi giày cũ của con trên kệ. Ông giữ đôi giày đó từ năm Davide mười sáu tuổi. Ông nói ông không uỷ mị, nhưng ông giữ. Đó có lẽ là bằng chứng cho một lý thuyết nhỏ của ông: rằng ông chưa từng nuôi dạy 'một bác sĩ'. Ông đã nuôi dạy một con người mà, trong những thứ khác, đã trở thành bác sĩ.

Rồi Marco quay lại với chiếc giày ông đang vá. Luôn còn một chiếc giày nữa.

questions.prompt: How long has Marco been a cobbler?
questions.explanation: 'He has been a cobbler for forty-one years, since he started as an apprentice for his father at twenty-one.'

questions.prompt: What does his son Davide do?
options: He is a lung specialist (pulmonologist) at the main hospital in Bologna.
He has taken over the workshop.
questions.explanation: 'He is a pulmonologist at the main hospital in Bologna — a specialist in lungs.'

questions.prompt: What does Marco mean when he says people's question 'assumes the answer'?
options: Their question expects him to say yes — and to mean a specific kind of yes (that his son rose above him).
He does not understand the question.
He has never been asked the question.
questions.explanation: 'They mean: are you proud that your son left the workshop and became a doctor? It is a question that assumes the answer.'

questions.prompt: What is Marco's own answer about pride?
options: He is not proud at all.
He is proud of Davide; he has always been proud — and a son becoming a doctor is not better than a father being a cobbler.
He stopped being proud years ago.
questions.explanation: He gives both halves: he is proud, AND he refuses the comparison.

questions.prompt: What does Marco's quote 'I do not measure children with a ruler that was given to me by other people' mean?
options: He literally does not own a ruler.
He refuses to judge his child by a scale of success that was handed to him by society.
He thinks children should not be measured at all.
He thinks his own father's ruler is the best.
questions.explanation: The 'ruler' is a metaphor for an external scale of success.
questions.hint: Look at the word 'ruler' — is it literal or a comparison?

questions.prompt: What does Marco NOT deny about the doctor's life?
options: That it is harder to get to, better paid, and more visible in the town
questions.explanation: 'Marco is not denying that the doctor's life is harder to get to, or better paid, or more visible in the town.'

options: A comparison — he refuses to say one life is 'a step above' the other.
The fact of his own work.
questions.explanation: 'He is denying a comparison. He is refusing to say that one life is a step above the other.'

questions.prompt: How often do Marco and Davide see each other?
questions.explanation: 'Davide and Marco still see each other every Sunday.'

questions.prompt: What do they talk about when they eat together?
options: A cousin moving back from Milan, Marco's mother at eighty-eight, a neighbour's leaking roof
questions.explanation: 'They talk about a cousin who is moving back from Milan, about Marco's mother who is now eighty-eight, about a neighbour's leaking roof.'

questions.prompt: Why has Marco kept Davide's old shoes from when he was sixteen?
options: He says he is not sentimental, but keeps them as the quiet proof that he was 'raising a person who, among other things, became a doctor.'
questions.explanation: 'He says he is not sentimental, but he keeps them. They are the proof, perhaps, of his small theory…'

questions.prompt: Which of these is Marco's 'small theory' about raising children?
options: He was never raising a doctor — he was raising a person who, among other things, became a doctor.
Children should choose their parents' work.
questions.hint: Look at the long sentence about 'raising.'

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: A parent can refuse the ranking 'my child rose above me' without refusing pride or love — and that refusal can itself be a kind of teaching.
Cobblers should always pass the trade to their children.
questions.explanation: The essay's core is Marco's refusal of the comparison, while still being proud and present.
questions.hint: Look at what Marco does and doesn't say.

blanks.explanation: Between a fruit shop and a barber.
A ruler — a scale of measurement.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Marco grants every practical difference between his son's life and his own, but refuses the moral comparison. Is there a comparison you have quietly made between your life and a relative's — child, parent, sibling — that, looked at carefully, is also using someone else's ruler? What would it cost you to put the ruler down?
